description:
You are a performance-tuning engineer who has just collected raw latency logs from two micro-services.  
    The logs are already present on the machine.

    1. Existing data locations  
       • /home/user/app_logs/serviceA.log  
       • /home/user/app_logs/serviceB.log  

       Each file is plain text, one entry per line, with the exact 3-column, white-space-separated layout below:

           <ISO-8601-timestamp> <response_time_ms> <status_code>

         Example (spacing is one ASCII space only):
           2023-01-01T00:00:00Z 123 200

    2. Your tasks  
       a. Generate a concise CSV performance summary named  
          /home/user/output/perf_summary.log  
          for every *.log file in /home/user/app_logs/.  
          • The file MUST include a header line exactly as shown below.  
          • One subsequent line per service, in the same order that the
            underlying *.log files are sorted alphabetically by filename.  
          • Numeric fields are integers, except error_rate_percent, which must keep one decimal place.  
          • The five comma-separated columns are:

              service_name,avg_ms,max_ms,min_ms,error_rate_percent

          -> Example of correct layout (do **not** add spaces):

              service_name,avg_ms,max_ms,min_ms,error_rate_percent
              serviceA,133,160,110,20.0
              serviceB,92,100,85,25.0

       b. Detect latency anomalies (response_time_ms > 150) across all logs and write them
          to /home/user/output/anomalies.log with the following pipe-delimited format
          (NO header line, NO surrounding spaces):

              <service_name>|<timestamp>|<response_time_ms>|<status_code>

          Example of correct, single-line result:

              serviceA|2023-07-01T12:00:07Z|160|503

    3. Requirements & constraints  
       • Do NOT modify anything inside /home/user/app_logs/.  
       • Create the /home/user/output directory if it does not yet exist.  
       • You are free to use one-liners, background jobs ( & ), GNU parallel, subshells, temporary files, etc., as long as the final artefacts exactly match the required formats above.  
       • When finished, both /home/user/output/*.log files must be present with
         the precise contents and ordering described.  
       • Produce any console output you find useful, but only the existence and
         content of the two files will be graded.  
       • Keep file permissions at their default umask-controlled user values
         (root access is unavailable).

    The automated tests will:  
       1. Verify that /home/user/output/perf_summary.log exists and matches the exact CSV structure and values.  
       2. Verify that /home/user/output/anomalies.log exists and contains only the expected anomaly lines.  
       3. Ensure no unexpected files were altered under /home/user/app_logs/.
